title: Why Relaxation Feels Threatening After Complex Trauma
researcherTopic: yoga and consciousness, the mechanics of the mind
metaDescription: For survivors of complex trauma, relaxation can feel like a threat. Discover why your nervous system resists safety and how to gently guide it toward rest.
imageDescription: A delicate, handcrafted ceramic bowl, repaired with shimmering gold in the kintsugi style, rests on a rustic wooden surface. Warm, gentle morning light illuminates the intricate golden cracks, symbolizing the beauty and strength found in healing. The scene evokes a sense of peace, resilience, and the quiet dignity of a mended past.
htmlContent:
Nobody warns you that healing complex trauma means the nervous system must unlearn the very strategies that kept it alive. The hypervigilance that scans every room for threats, the bracing for impact that lives in the shoulders, the shallow breath that keeps you ready to flee... these were not mistakes. They were adaptations. It panics. Because for a nervous system wired for danger, the absence of a threat feels like the most terrifying threat of all. It is the silence after the bomb drops, the eerie calm before the storm. It is the feeling of being exposed, unguarded, and utterly unprepared for the other shoe that has always, eventually, dropped.

The Architecture of Bracing

For those who have navigated the treacherous terrain of complex trauma, the body becomes a fortress. Every muscle, every nerve fiber is recruited into a state of perpetual readiness. This is not a conscious choice; it is a deeply ingrained physiological reality. The nervous system, in its real and ancient wisdom, has learned that the world is not a safe place. And so, it builds a fortress of tension, a suit of armor forged in the fires of past experience. This armor is heavy, it is exhausting, but it is also familiar. It is, in a strange and painful way, home.

A client once described this to me as feeling like a tightly coiled spring. “For years,” she said, “that coil was the only thing that held me together. The thought of letting it go, of unspooling… it feels like I would simply fall apart.” It turns out to be the paradox of healing. The very tension that causes so much suffering is also the scaffolding that has kept us upright. It is to step into a vast and terrifying emptiness, without any guarantee that we will find solid ground on the other side. We must learn to build a new kind of structure, one based not on tension, but on a felt sense of internal safety. A structure that can bend without breaking, that can yield without collapsing. This is the slow, patient work of rebuilding the foundations of the self.

The Tyranny of the Vagus Nerve

The vagus nerve, that great wandering nerve that connects the brain to the body, is the superhighway of our felt experience. It is the primary channel through which the brain and the gut communicate, the pathway along which the sensations of safety and danger travel. But in a nervous system shaped by complex trauma, this rhythm is broken. The vagus nerve becomes, in effect, a tyrant, locking the body into a state of chronic hyperarousal or hypoarousal.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link).

Here's what I want you to notice right now. As you're reading this, your nervous system is doing something. Maybe your shoulders crept up toward your ears. Maybe your breath got shallow. That's not a problem - that's information. Your body is telling you something about how safe it feels to take this in.

Stephen Porges talks about this in his polyvagal work. The nervous system doesn't wait for your conscious mind to decide whether something is threatening. It's already made that call before you finish reading a sentence. Your neuroception - that below-conscious threat detection system - is running 24/7. It doesn't take breaks. It doesn't sleep. And it was shaped by every experience you've ever had, especially the ones you can't remember.

I think about this a lot. How much of what we call "personality" is actually just a nervous system doing its best with the wiring it got. How much of what we call "overreacting" is actually a perfectly calibrated response to a world that once was genuinely dangerous. Your wiring isn't broken. It's doing exactly what it learned to do.

The Ghost in the Machine

Here is the thing, though.

The Seduction of Stillness

There is a deep and often overlooked difference between relaxation and stillness. Relaxation is a state of release, a letting go of tension. Stillness, on the other hand, is a state of presence, a deep and abiding connection to the present moment. Stillness, however, can be a lifeline. It is a way of being with the intensity of our experience without being consumed by it. It is a way of finding the eye of the storm, the quiet center in the midst of the chaos.

When we can find this stillness within ourselves, the need for the armor of tension begins to fall away. We no longer need to brace ourselves against the world, because we have found a place of refuge within. We no longer need to fear the intensity of our own experience, because we have learned that we are vast enough to contain it all. It is a slow, patient, and often messy process of re-wiring the nervous system, of teaching the body a new way of being in the world. It is the work of a lifetime, the great and glorious work of coming home to ourselves. It is the road from a life of reaction to a life of conscious response.

The Unfolding Path

There is no map for this path, no one-size-fits-all solution. The path of healing from complex trauma is a deeply personal one, a path that unfolds one step, one breath, one moment of courageous presence at a time. It is the act of choosing, again and again, to meet our own experience with a gentle and unwavering attention.

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link).

As we walk this path, we begin to discover that we are not, in fact, broken. We are not damaged goods. We are, and have always been, whole. The trauma did not break us. The work of healing is the work of gently and patiently lifting these veils, of remembering the wholeness that has been here all along. It is the work of reclaiming the parts of ourselves that we have been forced to abandon, of integrating the fragmented pieces of our experience into a new and more coherent whole. It is the sacred and essential work of becoming who we truly are. For those on this path, it can be helpful to explore the concept of the return to wholeness.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why does my body tense up when I try to meditate?

What we find here is a common and understandable experience for those with a history of complex trauma. Meditation, in its essence, is an invitation to be still and to turn our attention inward. For a nervous system that is wired for danger, this can feel like a real threat. The stillness can be interpreted as a state of vulnerability, and the inward focus can bring us into contact with the very sensations and emotions that we have spent a lifetime trying to avoid. The key is not to fight this tension, but to meet it with a gentle and curious awareness. To notice it, to acknowledge it, and to allow it to be there without judgment. Over time, as the nervous system begins to learn that it is safe to be still, this tension will begin to soften.

Let me tell you something that took me years to understand. The brain doesn't store trauma the way it stores your grocery list. It doesn't file it neatly in the hippocampus with a date stamp and a narrative arc. Trauma gets fragmented. Scattered across neural networks like shrapnel. A sound here. A body sensation there. A feeling with no story attached to it.

Bessel van der Kolk's research showed this clearly - traumatic memories live in the body, in the right hemisphere, in the amygdala. They bypass the language centers entirely. That's why you can't "think" your way out of a flashback. That's why someone can tell you "you're safe now" and your body doesn't believe a word of it.

Notice what's happening in your body right now. Is there tightness somewhere? A heaviness? Maybe a numbness that feels like nothing but is actually something. Your body has been holding this story for you. It's been doing that job faithfully, even when it cost you everything.

Is it possible to ever feel truly safe in my body again?

Yes, it is. But it is important to understand that the path to a felt sense of safety is not a linear one. It is a road of spirals, of ups and downs, of two steps forward and one step back. There will be moments of striking connection and safety, and there will be moments when the old fears and tensions resurface. It is simply the nature of the healing process. The key is to continue to cultivate the resources that support a felt sense of safety - practices like grounding, orienting, and co-regulation with a trusted other. And to remember that every moment of safety, no matter how fleeting, is a victory. It is a new neural pathway being forged, a new possibility being written into the story of your body.

For calming an overactive nervous system, you could also try Pure Encapsulations GABA (paid link).

How can I tell the difference between relaxation and dissociation?

What we find here is a crucial distinction to make. Relaxation is a state of embodied presence. It is a feeling of being connected to your body, to your breath, to the present moment. There is a sense of warmth, of softness, of ease. Dissociation, on the other hand, is a state of disconnection. It is a protective mechanism, a way of escaping from overwhelming sensations or emotions. One of the key differences is the quality of your awareness. In a state of relaxation, your awareness is clear and present. In a state of dissociation, your awareness is fuzzy, foggy, or absent altogether. Learning to track your own internal state, to notice the subtle shifts between presence and disconnection, is a vital skill on the healing path.

Think about that for a second.

What if I can't even imagine what it would feel like to be relaxed?

What emerges is a very real and valid experience for many survivors of complex trauma. When the nervous system has been in a state of chronic activation for years, or even decades, the concept of relaxation can feel completely alien. It can be like trying to imagine a color you have never seen. In this case, the work is not to try to force a state of relaxation, but to start with something much smaller, much more accessible. To savor these moments, to magnify them, to let them land in your awareness. This is how we begin to build a new vocabulary in the body, a new set of experiences that can, over time, grow into a genuine and sustainable capacity for rest. And for those who want to explore this further, I invite you to connect with the work we do at Kalesh.love, where we examine deeper into the mechanics of consciousness and the path to embodied liberation.

The path out of the shattered armor of complex trauma is not a race to a finish line, but a slow and tender homecoming. It is the process of unlearning a lifetime of bracing, of vigilance, of waiting for the other shoe to drop. It is the radical act of teaching an exhausted nervous system that the war is, finally, over. It is the quiet miracle of returning to the body, not as a battlefield, but as a sanctuary. A place of wisdom, of resilience, of deep and unwavering grace.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Nature Made Magnesium Glycinate 400mg (paid link).

Do the thing your body has been asking for.
